summary: Crystal‑clear soundboard captures an incendiary 10/12/68 show – blistering Dark Star, a monster Other One suite and one of the all‑time great Elevenths.
review: Listeners rave that this soundboard recording is virtually flawless, with crystal‑clear instrument separation and just enough vintage warmth to feel like you’re in the Avalon Ballroom. The performance is legendary: a soaring Dark Star, a ferocious Other One suite, an elemental Eleven, plus scorching St. Stephen, Cryptical→Other One and New Potato Caboose, all delivered with relentless energy and tight interplay. Minor mix quirks (loud shaker on Dark Star) are the only blemish mentioned.

ai_rating.rationale: Over 70 reviews, virtually all 5‑star, praising both musical performance and recording fidelity; a few minor criticisms do not detract from the overwhelmingly positive consensus.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Crystal‑clear soundstage, superb instrument balance, warm vintage tape character. One reviewer noted an overly loud shaker on Dark Star; another mentioned slight volume swings but overall the mix is superb.

show_quality.setlist_flow: High‑energy, seamless transitions – the jam after New Potato flows into a feedback climax, and the second set rides an unstoppable wave of psychedelic intensity.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Garcia’s guitar tone and solos are repeatedly called "perfect", "ripping" and "beautiful"; his vocal delivery on Dark Star and Death Don't Have No Mercy is highlighted as a standout.
band_member_comments.Phil: Lesh’s bass foundation, especially on The Eleven, is described as “tight”, “groovy” and the basis for one of the best Elevenths ever heard.
band_member_comments.Bob: Weir’s rhythm guitar and vocals receive praise; his singing on New Potato Caboose is noted as a highlight despite occasional vocal weakness elsewhere.
band_member_comments.Keys: Keyboard absence is remarked upon (no organ), but when present the piano/organ work is praised for its psychedelic texture.
band_member_comments.Drums: Bill Kreutzmann’s drumming and the drum‑>jam‑>feedback segment are lauded for their power; a few note a slightly weak drums transition, but overall the percussion drives the show.
